segments.inferno-21-g52: Quel is a demonstrative pronoun meaning 'that one' (referring back to a previously mentioned creature). s' is the clitic reflexive si contracted before the vowel, and attuffò is the passato remoto (3rd singular) of attuffare meaning 'plunged (himself)'; together they mean 'that one plunged (himself)'.
segments.inferno-21-g53: e is the coordinating conjunction 'and'. tornò is passato remoto (3rd singular) of tornare 'returned/turned back'; sù is the adverb 'up'. convolto is a past participle used adjectivally ('rolled/twisted up'), so the phrase means 'and he came back up, rolled up'.
segments.inferno-21-g54: ma = 'but'. i demon is the plural article + noun 'the demons' (demon is an older/poetic spelling). che introduces a relative clause 'who/which'. del ponte = di + il 'of the bridge'. avean is the archaic imperfect form (avean = avevano) 'they had'. coperchio is the noun 'cover/covering' and is the complement of avean: 'the demons who had a covering of the bridge'.
segments.inferno-21-g55: gridar here introduces direct speech; in Dante an infinitival or truncated narrative form like gridar functions to mean 'they cried/shouted' and immediately precedes the quotation.
segments.inferno-21-g56: Qui = 'here/at this place'. non ha loco is an idiom: ha (present indicative 3rd sing. of avere) + loco 'place' = 'has no place' → 'there is no place'. il Santo Volto is the noun phrase 'the Holy Face' (Volto = 'face'); the whole line means 'There is no place here for the Holy Face'.
segments.inferno-21-g57: qui = 'here'. si nuota uses the clitic si to form an impersonal construction ('one swims' / 'people swim'); nuota is present indicative of nuotare 'to swim'. altrimenti = 'otherwise/differently'. che introduces a comparison ('than') and nel Serchio specifies the River Serchio (local reference): 'here one swims differently than in the Serchio.'
segments.inferno-21-g58: Però is an adverb meaning 'therefore/however/then' used here to signal a consequence or admonition following the shout.
segments.inferno-21-g59: se introduces a conditional clause ('if'). tu is the stressed 2nd-person pronoun 'you'. non negates the verb. vuo’ is the apocopated form of vuoi (present indicative 'you want'); the apostrophe marks the dropped vowel (poetic spelling). di nostri graffi = 'of/from our scratches' (nostri = 'our', graffi = 'scratches/claws/attacks'), meaning 'if you don't want to get scratched by us'.
segments.inferno-21-g60: non far is a negative imperative/admonition 'do not do' (far is the infinitive form used here in poetry to express the imperative or prohibition). sopra la pegola = 'on/over the pitch' (pegola = pitch/tar used on the plank). soverchio is a postpositive adjective meaning 'excessive' → 'do not put too much pitch on the plank.'
segments.inferno-21-g61: Poi = 'then/next'. l’addentar combines the clitic lo (l') attached to the infinitive addentar (to bite into/attack) — literally 'to bite him'. con più di cento raffi = 'with more than a hundred blows/strokes' (più di = 'more than', raffi = sudden strokes/jerks). The infinitive with clitic is a poetic narrative way to state the action they undertake.
segments.inferno-21-g62: disser is the contracted passato remoto (they said) — a poetic/truncated form of dissero — used to introduce direct speech ('they said:').
segments.inferno-21-g63: Coverto is an adjective 'covered/hidden'. convien (archaic conviene) is an impersonal present 'it is necessary' and takes a that-clause introduced by che. qui = 'here'. balli is present subjunctive (3rd singular) of ballare after convien che, meaning 'that he dance/struggle here' — subjunctive marks necessity or obligation.
segments.inferno-21-g64: sì che is a result/connective phrase 'so that', linking the necessity just stated to the following conditional instruction.
segments.inferno-21-g65: se = 'if' (introduces the conditional). puoi is present indicative 2nd singular of potere 'you can/are able' — 'if you can'. This addresses the listener and sets the condition for the following imperative/exhortation.
segments.inferno-21-g66: nascosamente is an adverb 'secretly/stealthily'. accaffi is an archaic/poetic 2nd-person form (used like an imperative or exhortation) meaning 'snatch/grab' (from accaffare/accappare); together with 'se puoi' it means 'if you can, secretly snatch him'.
segments.inferno-21-g24: Comma punctuation marking a pause before the clause; not a lexical element to interpret here.
segments.inferno-21-g25: mi (accusative clitic) = 'me'; trasse is passato remoto (3rd sg.) of trarre, here 'drew/pulled'; a sé = 'to himself' (a + reflexive sé) indicating direction toward the guide; del = contraction di + il and here means 'from the' (indicating origin or source); loco = 'place, spot'; dov’ (dove) = 'where' (relative/adverbial); io stava = imperfect tense 'I was / I stood' (stare used to indicate location). The whole phrase means that he pulled the narrator toward himself out of the spot where the narrator had been standing.
segments.inferno-21-g26: Allor = 'then' (temporal adverb). mi volsi = passato remoto 1st-person reflexive of volgersi/volgere: 'I turned (myself)'; come = 'as/like' introducing a simile; l’uom = 'the man'; cui tarda di veder = relative clause: cui is a relative dative ('to whom' / 'who'), tarda is 3rd-singular present 'is slow' (literally 'it is slow to him'), di veder = 'to see' (infinitive introduced by di); quel che li convien fuggire = 'that which it is necessary for him to flee' — convien is impersonal 'it is necessary' and li is the dative pronoun referring to the man; e cui paura sùbita sgagliarda = 'and whose sudden fear weakens him' (cui = 'whose', paura sùbita = 'sudden fear', sgagliarda is an archaic/poetic verb meaning 'renders weak/feeble' or 'overcomes'). The clause is a sustained simile comparing the speaker's quick turning to a frightened man's hesitant perception.
segments.inferno-21-g27: che = conjunction 'that/which' used here to link the simile to the following explanatory clause; functions as a connective particle.
segments.inferno-21-g28: per veder = 'in order to see' or 'to get a look' — per + infinitive expresses purpose or motive; here parenthetical: 'who, for the purpose of seeing...'.
segments.inferno-21-g29: non = negation; indugia (3rd sg.) = 'hesitates/lingers/ delays' (from indugiare); ’l is the clitic article/pronoun (here contracted before the infinitive) that stands with partire; partire = infinitive 'to depart' — the phrase means 'does not delay in departing' or 'does not hesitate to go off'. Note the archaic clitic placement (’l) and the infinitive used with that clitic.
segments.inferno-21-g30: e vidi = 'and I saw' (vidi = passato remoto of vedere); dietro a noi = 'behind us' (dietro + a + noi); un diavol nero = 'a black devil' (noun phrase); correndo (gerund) + su per lo scoglio + venire (infinitive) form a motion clause: correndo = 'running', su per lo scoglio = 'up along/on the crag', venire = infinitive 'to come' used with vidi to mean 'I saw him come (while) running up the crag.' In Italian, vedeo + infinitive often reports perceived motion ('I saw him coming').
segments.inferno-21-g31: Ahi = interjection 'ah' or 'alas'; quant’ (quant = quanto) = exclamatory 'how' or 'how much'; elli = archaic third-person pronoun (here 'he/it' referring to the devil); era = imperfect 'was'; ne l’ aspetto = 'in the appearance' (ne = 'in' / 'in that'); fero = adjective 'fierce' or 'ferocious' modifying aspetto. The line is an exclamation about the creature's fierce look.
segments.inferno-21-g32: e = 'and'; quanto = 'how' (intensifier); mi parea = imperfect of parere with dative clitic 'mi' = 'seemed to me' (parere in imperfect 'seemed'); ne l’atto acerbo = 'in the harsh/harsh-looking gesture/ bearing' (ne = 'in', l’atto = 'the act/gesture/bearing', acerbo used figuratively 'harsh, stern'). The clause continues the speaker's observation about the devil's look and manner.
segments.inferno-21-g33: con l’ali aperte = 'with his wings open/spread' (con + noun phrase; aperte = past participle used adjectivally); e sovra i piè leggero = 'and light upon his feet' (sovra = 'over/above/on', i piè = 'the feet', leggero = adjective 'light/nimble' describing his step). The line gives visual details of the devil's stance and movement.
segments.inferno-21-g85: Innanzi che introduces a temporal clause meaning "before" and therefore requires the subjunctive; l’uncin is the poetically contracted article + noun (l' + uncin = "the hook"); vostro is the possessive modifying uncin; mi is the direct-object clitic "me"; pigli is the present-subjunctive (older form) 3rd person singular of pigliare, used after innanzi che to express an action that would occur after the main clause.
segments.inferno-21-g86: Traggasi is an impersonal/imperative form with the enclitic si (literally 'let himself be drawn' or more idiomatically 'let one step forward'); avante = 'forward'; l’un di voi = 'one of you' (subject of the imperative/hortative); che introduces a relative/subordinate clause; m' (here the clitic before oda) stands for mi = 'me' (the data provided for senses is mismatched); oda is present subjunctive (or hortative subjunctive) 3rd person singular meaning 'may hear' or 'hear him'.
segments.inferno-21-g87: e poi = 'and then'; d' is the contracted preposition di used here like English 'about'/'concerning'; arruncigliarmi is the infinitive arruncigliare + enclitic mi ('to hook/claw me'); si consigli is reflexive (consigliarsi) in a hortative/imperative sense for the same l’un previously named — i.e. 'let him consider/consult (about it)'; consigli here is a present-subjunctive/imperative-form used to express that exhortation.
segments.inferno-21-g88: Tutti is the subject 'all/everyone'; gridaron is the passato remoto (past historic) 3rd-person-plural of gridare, a narrative past meaning 'they shouted/they cried out'. The colon introduces the quoted exclamation.
segments.inferno-21-g89: Vada is the present subjunctive of andare used here as a jussive/imperative ('let him go' / 'go!'); Malacoda is the proper name of the speaker. The subjunctive 3rd person is commonly used in older/poetic Italian to issue commands or wishes about a third party.
segments.inferno-21-g90: This segment is a punctuation mark (semicolon) linking the previous speech to the subsequent narrative action; it separates clauses and has no lexical items to analyze.
segments.inferno-21-g91: per ch' is a compressed form (per che / perchè) meaning 'wherefore' or 'so that' / 'for which reason'; un si mosse = uno si mosse, where si mosse is the passato remoto (3rd singular) of muovere with the reflexive/impersonal si ('one moved' / 'one stirred'); e li altri stetter fermi = 'and the others stood still' (stetter is passato remoto of stare or a literary form meaning 'they stood/stayed'); e venne a lui dicendo = 'and came to him, saying', with venne (passato remoto of venire) + the present participle dicendo 'saying'.
segments.inferno-21-g92: Che li approda? is an idiomatic interrogative: Che = 'what'; li is a clitic meaning 'to them' (dative/indirect object) referring back to the others; approda here is used in the sense 'to avail' or 'to profit' (what does it avail them?), so approda is 3rd-person-singular present used impersonally with the dative clitic.
segments.inferno-21-g93: Punctuation: the single period ends the previous sentence in the manuscript/verse.
segments.inferno-21-g34: L’ is the contracted definite article (il) before a vowel; here it marks the noun omero. Omero normally means 'shoulder' (or the upper arm/shoulder-bone) in older Italian; suo is a possessive adjective agreeing in gender/number with omero ('his/its'). Word order is article + noun + possessive, a regular noun phrase.
segments.inferno-21-g35: ch’ is the clitic form of che, used here as a relative pronoun 'which/who' introducing the clause that modifies omero. era is the imperfect indicative of essere ('was'). aguto is an adjectival past participle/ adjective meaning 'sharp/lean/pointed' in older usage (here 'lean' or 'gaunt'); superbo is an adjective 'proud/haughty'. The coordinate adjectives agree with omero and are linked by the conjunction e.
segments.inferno-21-g36: carcava is the imperfect of carcare (to load, weigh down, or bear) and governs the following direct object un peccator. The indefinite article un precedes peccator ('a sinner'). con ambo l’anche is a prepositional phrase: con 'with' + ambo 'both' + l’anche 'the hips' (anche here is a body-part noun). The verb phrase describes continuous/habitual bearing (imperfect).
segments.inferno-21-g37: e is the coordinating conjunction 'and'. quei is a demonstrative/pronoun 'that one' or 'the latter' referring back to the previously mentioned person. tenea is the imperfect of tenere ('held' or 'kept'). de’ piè is an archaic/poetic form = 'of the feet' (de' = di + i); ghermito is a past participle/ adjective meaning 'seized, clutched' (here describing how the sinew was held). ’l nerbo = il nerbo ('the sinew' or tendon); word order is typical: subject implied, verb, and object phrases.
segments.inferno-21-g38: Del is the contracted preposition di + il meaning 'of/from the' and introduces the prepositional phrase 'from our bridge'. nostro is the possessive adjective 'our' modifying ponte 'bridge'. disse is the passato remoto (or simple past) of dire — 'he said'. The phrase functions as a frame for the reported exclamation.
segments.inferno-21-g39: O is a vocative/interjection 'O' (used to call or address). Malebranche is a proper name (literally 'Evil Claws' in Dante) addressed directly; the comma and vocative signal direct address to that group/name.
segments.inferno-21-g40: ecco is an interjection meaning 'behold' or 'here is' used to draw attention. un de li anzïan = 'one of the anzïan' where un = 'a/one', de li = 'of the' and anzïan is an old/official 'elder' or 'magistrate' (here: a member/elder of the guild/association). di Santa Zita = 'of Saint Zita' (di + proper name) identifies which elders are meant.
segments.inferno-21-g41: Mettetel is the imperative plural of mettere with enclitic pronoun -lo (-l) and -te? Actually mettetel = mettete + lo (put him), so it's a command 'put him'. sotto is the adverb/preposition 'under' or 'beneath' specifying location. Imperative + object is typical word order.
segments.inferno-21-g42: ch’ abbreviates che used here as a causal conjunction 'for/because'. i’ is the emphatic subject pronoun 'I'. torno is the present or imperfect shape here meaning 'I return' / 'I am returning' (context implies immediate future/intent). per anche is 'also/too' (per + anche amplifies 'I return as well') and a quella terra = 'to that land', preposition a + demonstrative + noun.
segments.inferno-21-g43: che is a relative pronoun 'which' introducing a clause describing quella terra. n’ is the clitic form of ne meaning 'of it' or 'thereof' in 'n’è ben fornita' = 'is well supplied with it/them'. è is the present of essere 'is'. ben is an adverb 'well'. fornita is a past participle used adjectivally 'supplied/provided', agreeing with terra.
segments.inferno-21-g44: ogn’ is the apocopated form of ogni 'every'. uom is the archaic form of uomo 'man'. v’ is the clitic form of vi/ci meaning 'there' (used in the impersonal/existential construction v’è 'there is'). è is 'is'. barattier (barattiero) is a noun 'briber' or 'corrupt official' (one who takes bribes). The clause states an existential fact about that place.
segments.inferno-21-g45: fuor che is an idiom meaning 'except that' or simply 'except'. Bonturo is a proper name (the one exception named). The semicolon separates the explanatory aside that follows.
segments.inferno-21-g46: del is the contraction di + il 'of the'. no here means 'no' as a noun (a negative vote/refusal); 'del no' = 'as for the
segments.inferno-21-g47: per is the preposition 'for' or 'because of'. li is the plural definite article 'the' (here before denar). denar (denaro) means 'money' or 'coins' and by extension 'bribes'; it is the object of the preposition 'per' indicating motive or exchange.
segments.inferno-21-g48: vi is an adverb/clitic meaning 'there' or 'in that place'. si is the impersonal/reflexive clitic used to form a passive/impersonal construction. fa is the present of fare 'makes/does' and ita is an adverb 'so/thus'. Together vi si fa ita means roughly 'there it is done so' or idiomatically 'there the (negative) decision is made (i.e. bought)'.
segments.inferno-21-g49: Là giù is an adverbial phrase 'there down' (i.e., 'down there'). ’l is the clitic form of lo (the direct-object pronoun) attached in writing; buttò is the passato remoto of buttare 'he threw' (here 'he cast him down'). The sequence describes a completed action.
segments.inferno-21-g50: e is 'and'. per lo scoglio duro is a prepositional phrase: per 'toward/along' (here 'toward') + lo scoglio 'the rock' + duro 'hard'. si volse is the reflexive passato remoto of volgersi 'to turn oneself' (he turned around/away). The reflexive si marks the subject's own motion.
segments.inferno-21-g51: e mai non fu is the negative past ('and never was'). mastino here should mean 'mastiff' (a fierce dog); sciolto is a past participle/adjective 'loosed/released' (literally 'set free' or 'let loose'). con tanta fretta 'with such haste' modifies the following infinitive a seguitar 'to follow/pursue'. lo furo: lo is the definite article 'the' and furo here refers to 'the thief' (an archaic noun), so the whole phrase means 'to pursue the thief.' The clause compares the speed of the pursuit to that of any dog let loose.
segments.inferno-21-g67: This clause uses a causative periphrasis: 'i cuoci ... fanno attuffare' = 'the cooks make (someone) plunge (something)'. The indirect object 'a’ lor vassalli' (a + their vassals) indicates who is made to do the plunging. Word order places the prepositional phrase 'in mezzo la caldaia' and the direct object 'la carne con li uncin' after the verb phrase; 'li uncin' is an archaic/poetic plural for 'hooks'.
segments.inferno-21-g68: 'Perché' here introduces a purpose/lest clause rather than a plain question (so that / lest), and it governs the present subjunctive 'galli' (third-person singular) — an archaic/poetic verb meaning 'to float/bob up'. The negation 'non' prevents the floating: 'so that it not float'.
segments.inferno-21-g69: 'Lo buon maestro' is the subject with the archaic article 'lo' before an adjective + noun. 'Acciò che' is an older form meaning 'so that' (introducing a purpose clause). 'Non si paia' is impersonal/reflective ('si' used impersonally) with subjunctive 'paia' ('seem/appear'), and the subordinate 'che tu ci sia' ('that you are here') uses the present subjunctive 'sia' after 'paia'. 'Ci' is locative ('there'/'here among them').
segments.inferno-21-g70: 'Mi disse' is a simple narrative insertion: 'disse' is passato remoto (he said) and 'mi' is the indirect object 'to me'. The phrase marks reported speech.
segments.inferno-21-g71: 'Giù t’acquatta' is an imperative command: 'giù' = 'down', and 't’' is the clitic object 'ti' attached to the verb 'acquatta' (imperative 2nd person singular of 'acquattare') = 'crouch down / hide yourself'. 'Dopo uno scheggio' is a locative phrase 'behind a splinter (of rock)', with 'dopo' = 'behind'.
segments.inferno-21-g72: This is a purpose clause introduced by 'ch’' (= 'che') meaning 'so that' or 'that'. 'Alcun schermo' = 'some shelter/screen', and 't’ aia' is an instance of the present subjunctive 'aia' (here functioning as a verb meaning 'may have' = subjunctive of 'avere') with clitic 't’' (to you): 'that you may have some shelter'. Note: the provided senses for 'aia' list only a noun sense (yard), so the verbal subjunctive sense is not among them.
segments.inferno-21-g73: 'E per nulla offension che mi sia fatta' means 'and for no offense to be done to me' / 'lest any offence befall me'. 'Per nulla' = 'for nothing / in no way'; 'offension' = 'offense/injury'; the clause 'che mi sia fatta' uses present subjunctive 'sia' with the past participle 'fatta' to express a potential/passive action (that might be done to me).
segments.inferno-21-g74: 'Non temer tu' is an admonition: 'non temer' = 'do not fear' (imperative/negative imperative addressed to 'tu'), with the subject pronoun 'tu' placed after the verb for emphasis — an archaic/poetic word order.
segments.inferno-21-g75: 'Ch’i’ ho le cose conte' = 'for I have the matters counted/settled'. 'Ho' is present indicative 'I have'; 'le cose' = 'the matters/things'; 'conte' is an archaic past participle/adjectival use meaning 'counted' or 'settled' (the provided senses list does not include this participial/adjectival meaning). The clause explains why he tells the listener not to fear.
segments.inferno-21-g76: 'Perch’ altra volta fui a tal baratta' gives the reason: 'perch’' = 'because/since', 'altra volta' = 'another time/on another occasion', 'fui' is passato remoto 1st person 'I was/I took part', 'a tal baratta' = 'in such a bargain/deal' — 'fui a' = 'I was involved in'.
segments.inferno-21-g77: 'Poscia passò' is a past-tense narrative move: 'poscia' = 'afterwards/then' and 'passò' is passato remoto (he passed/crossed). 'Di là' = 'over there/on the other side', and 'dal' = 'da + il' ('from the'). The phrase 'co del ponte' names the part of the bridge he left/was on; the whole prepositional sequence marks origin/place.
segments.inferno-21-g78: 'E com’ el giunse' uses 'com’' (contraction of 'come') functioning here as 'when/as', followed by 'el giunse' (passato remoto 'he arrived/reached'). 'In su' = 'up onto/above', and 'la ripa sesta' = 'the sixth bank' with the numeral 'sesta' following the noun.
segments.inferno-21-g79: 'Mestier li fu d’aver sicura fronte' is an impersonal/experiential construction: 'mestier' = 'need/necessity', 'li' = 'to him' (dative clitic; archaic placement before the verb), and 'fu' (passato remoto of 'essere') gives 'it was necessary for him'. 'D’aver' = 'to have' (infinitive), and 'sicura fronte' = 'a steady/confident front/forehead' (i.e., to keep a steady face).
segments.inferno-21-g80: Con quel furore e con quella tempesta introduces means/instrument with repeated preposition 'con' ('with that fury and with that storm'). ch’ is the contracted form of che (relative 'which/who') introducing the relative clause. Escono is the present (historical/pictorial) tense 'they come out' used to describe an action in the scene; the phrase a dosso al poverello ('a dosso' + article + noun) literally places the action 'upon the back of the poor fellow'. Di sùbito ('di' + adverb) = 'suddenly/at once', and chiede (present) plus the interrogative ove introduces an indirect question 'asks where s(arresta)' — s’ (si) is the clitic reflexive inserted before the verb and arresta is present indicative 'stops/halts'.
segments.inferno-21-g81: Usciron is the archaic/poetic passato remoto form (uscirono) 'they came out' — shortened final -o dropped in verse. Quei di sotto = 'those from below' (quei + prepositional phrase di sotto). Al ponticello is the simple locative/directional phrase 'to the little bridge.' The line is a compact past-action clause continuing the narrative.
segments.inferno-21-g82: E volser contra lui tutt’ i runcigli: volser is another archaic past form (volsero) 'they turned' or 'they directed'; contra (against/toward) takes the object lui ('him'). Tutt’ is the elided form of tutti (tutt’) used for metrical reasons and governs the plural noun i runcigli ('the hooks/talons'), the things turned against him.
segments.inferno-21-g83: Ma is the adversative 'but'. El is the archaic subject pronoun (for egli/egli) meaning 'he' and gridò is passato remoto 'he cried/shouted.' Together they introduce his spoken reaction.
segments.inferno-21-g84: This is direct speech. Nessun di voi = 'no one of you/none among you.' Sia is the present subjunctive of essere used jussively (a prohibitive or hortatory force = 'let none of you be...'). Fello is an archaic/pejorative noun/adjective 'villain/treacherous one'; the whole phrase expresses a command/wish forbidding treachery.
segments.inferno-21-g1: An adverbial phrase: Così ('thus') sets the manner; di ponte in ponte is a repeated prepositional phrase meaning 'from bridge to bridge' (di = 'from' and in = 'in' used idiomatically to show movement/location from one bridge to another). Word order is poetic repetition for rhythm.
segments.inferno-21-g2: Gerund phrase + relative clause. 'altro parlando' (altro + gerund parlando) = 'speaking of other things'; che introduces a clause that modifies that activity: 'la mia comedìa cantar non cura' = 'my Comedy does not care to sing' (cantar = infinitive, cura = 'care' with negation non cura). Note the possessive with article 'la mia comedìa'.
segments.inferno-21-g3: Finite verb in passato remoto, 1st person plural: 'venimmo' = 'we came/arrived' (narrative past). The semicolon links this arrival to what follows.
segments.inferno-21-g4: Coordinating clause: 'e' = 'and'. 'tenavamo' is an imperfect form (poetic/archaic usage) meaning 'we kept/held' (here nautical: 'kept the crest/high point'); ’l is the contracted article 'il' (= the) before colmo. 'Colmo' = 'crest/summit' (the top of the passage).
segments.inferno-21-g5: Temporal clause 'quando' = 'when'. 'restammo' (passato remoto) = 'we stopped/paused'. 'per veder' = 'in order to see' (per + infinitive expresses purpose). 'l’altra fessura di Malebolge' is the direct object (the other fissure of Malebolge); 'e li altri pianti vani' apposes 'the other vain lamentations' (li is an archaic plural article).
segments.inferno-21-g6: 'e' = 'and'. 'vidila' is a past tense verb with an attached object pronoun: vidi + la → vidila = 'I saw it/her' (the enclitic -la attached to the past verb is a common poetic form). 'mirabilmente' is an adverb modifying 'oscura' = 'marvelously dark/obscure.'
segments.inferno-21-g7: 'Quale' introduces a simile ('as/like when'). 'ne l’arzanà de’ Viniziani' = 'in the arsenal of the Venetians' (ne = in; arzanà = arsenal/dockyard). 'bolle' is present (or narrative present) 'boils' and has as object 'la tenace pece' = 'the tenacious/sticky pitch'; 'a rimpalmare' (a + infinitive) expresses purpose 'to recaulk'; 'i legni lor non sani' = 'their unsound timbers/wood'.
segments.inferno-21-g8: Causal/explanatory 'ché' = 'because/for'. 'navicar non ponno' = 'they cannot sail' (navicar = to sail; ponno = can). 'in quella vece' = 'in that case/instance'; the following are a series of relative/indefinite clauses introduced by 'chi' ('who/one who'): 'chi fa suo legno novo' = 'some make a new ship/hull' and 'chi ristoppa le coste a quel che più viaggi fece' = 'some recaulk the ribs of the one that has voyaged most' (ristoppa = re-caulk; 'quel che più vïaggi fece' is a relative clause 'that which/that which has made more voyages').
segments.inferno-21-g9: Parallel clauses introduced by chi: 'chi ribatte da proda e chi da poppa' = 'some hammer/beat at (the planks) from the prow and some from the stern.' 'Ribatte' is a present/generic verb here; 'da proda/da poppa' indicate place (from the bow/from the stern).
segments.inferno-21-g10: 'altri fa remi e altri volge sarte' = 'others make oars and others adjust/turn the rigging.' 'Altri' = 'others'; 'fa' = 'make/do'; 'remi' = 'oars'; 'volge sarte' = 'turn/handle the sarti(e)' (sarte = ship's rigging/lines).
segments.inferno-21-g11: Specific tasks continued: 'chi terzeruolo e artimon rintoppa' = 'some patch the terzeruolo (small sail) and the artimon (mizzen)'. The verbs are generic/present; rintoppa = 'patch up/plug'. The dash signals the end of the inventory.
segments.inferno-21-g12: Simple demonstrative 'tal' = 'such' (refers back to the just-described mode of activity — 'such [is the bustle]').
segments.inferno-21-g13: Contrastive formula: 'non per foco ma per divin’ arte' = 'not by fire but by divine art/skill.' 'Non ... ma ...' makes the opposition; 'divin’' shows the elision for meter.
segments.inferno-21-g14: 'bollia là giuso una pegola spessa' = 'there below boiled a thick lump/blob of pitch.' 'Bollia' is a past narrative form ('was boiling' / 'boiled'); 'là giuso' = 'down there'; 'una pegola spessa' = 'a thick blob of pitch' (pegola = mass/patch of pitch).
segments.inferno-21-g15: Relative clause introduced by 'che' = 'which/that'. '’nviscava' (nviscare) is imperfect 'made sticky/entangled' and takes 'la ripa d’ogne parte' = 'the bank/shore on every side' as direct object (ogne parte = 'every side').
segments.inferno-21-g16: Simple declarative: 'I’ (io) = 'I' as subject; 'vedea' is the imperfect 'I was seeing' or 'I saw' (narrative imperfect); 'lei' = 'her/it' is the direct object (referring back to the pegola/fissure).
segments.inferno-21-g17: Contrast: 'ma' = 'but'. 'non vedëa in essa mai che ...' is a negative clause meaning 'I never saw in it anything but ...' ('mai' functions as 'ever/at any time' in a negative clause → 'never'). The subordinate clause 'che le bolle che ’l bollor levava' contains 'le bolle' = 'the bubbles' and 'che ’l bollor levava' = 'which the boiling raised' (levava = imperfect 'raised').
segments.inferno-21-g18: Infinitive phrase used as part of the descriptive content: 'e gonfiar tutta' = 'and to swell up entirely' (gonfiar = infinitive 'to swell'; tutta = 'completely/all' agreeing with feminine bubble). The infinitives here describe what those bubbles do (poetic coordination of forms).
segments.inferno-21-g19: 'e riseder compressa' continues the coordinated description: riseder here (poetic/archaic form) means 'to sit again / to settle back' (i.e., sink back down) and 'compressa' = 'compressed' describes their state when they settle. The verbs are presented in parallel (infinitive/poetic forms) to the previous 'gonfiar.'
segments.inferno-21-g20: 'Mentr’ io ... mirava' is a temporal subordinate: 'while I was gazing.' The apostrophe in 'Mentr’' is the elided 'mente' (mentre). 'fisamente' (adverb) = 'fixedly/intently' modifies 'mirava' (imperfect 'I was gazing').
segments.inferno-21-g21: Nominal phrase 'lo duca mio' = 'my guide/duke' in apposition to the subject of the next clause; 'lo' is the article and 'mio' the possessive.
segments.inferno-21-g22: 'dicendo' is a present participle/gerund meaning 'saying' and introduces the direct speech that follows; 'Guarda' is the imperative 'look' used in direct address.
segments.inferno-21-g23: Repetition of the imperative 'guarda!' for emphasis in the direct quotation; vocative/imperative use addressing the narrator or companions.
segments.inferno-21-g120: E is the coordinating conjunction “and,” linking this reply to what came before. Rispondien is a 3rd-person plural past form (archaic/imperfect/historical present feel in Dante) meaning “they answered.” The colon marks direct speech that follows.
segments.inferno-21-g121: Sì is the simple affirmative interjection “Yes,” here used to introduce the demons’ prompt reply; the comma shows the brief pause before the command that follows.
segments.inferno-21-g122: Fa che is a causative idiom: fa (imperative/present used to give an order) + che introduces a subordinate clause with the subjunctive to express “make/see that ….” Gliel’ is the clitic sequence gli + lo/la contracted (to him + it/him), marking the indirect and direct object together. Accocchi is the present subjunctive (or hortatory subjunctive) of accoccare ‘to lay/strike/bring down’ and completes the commanded subordinate clause: “make that he lay/strike it on him.”
segments.inferno-21-g123: Ma = “but.” Quel demonio is the noun phrase “that demon.” Che tenea sermone is a relative clause: che (who/which) + tenea (archaic imperfect of tenere, “was holding/keeping”) + sermone (a discourse/conversation), so “that demon who was holding conversation.” Col duca mio = con + il (col) + duca mio, “with my duke/guide.”
segments.inferno-21-g124: Si volse is reflexive + past verb (volse, passato remoto) meaning “he turned himself” → “he turned.” Tutto presto is an adverbial phrase “completely/very quickly,” with e disse (“and said”) continuing the narrative into direct speech.
segments.inferno-21-g125: Posa is an imperative/exhortation meaning “rest/stop/hold” (here an abrupt command to be quiet or to halt an action); the opening quotation mark shows the start of the demon’s spoken words.
segments.inferno-21-g126: The repeated posa reinforces the command as an interjection — a short emphatic order (“Stop, stop”).
segments.inferno-21-g127: Scarmiglione is a proper name (the name of a devil), here in direct address (vocative) ending the exclamation; the closing punctuation ends the quoted speech.
segments.inferno-21-g128: This segment is only the sentence-final full stop (punctuation) separating the lines of the poem; there are no lexical items to analyze.
segments.inferno-21-g129: Poi = “then/afterwards.” Disse is passato remoto third-person singular “he said.” A noi is the indirect object “to us,” so the whole clause is “Then he said to us:” introducing the following quoted statement.
segments.inferno-21-g130: Più oltre is an adverbial phrase meaning “any farther/no further”; andar is the infinitive “to go” and is the verbal idea governed by the impersonal construction non si può (“it is not possible/one cannot”). Per questo iscoglio = “through/over this rock,” with per meaning “through/over” and iscoglio “rock/crag.” The impersonal si + può expresses inability: “one cannot go farther.”
segments.inferno-21-g131: Però che introduces the reason (“because/for the reason that”); giace is present indicative “lies/is situated.” Tutto spezzato uses tutto as an adverb “completely” and spezzato (past participle) adjectivally “broken.” Al fondo = “at the bottom”; l’arco sesto = “the sixth arch.” The clause explains why one cannot proceed: the sixth arch lies completely broken at the bottom.
segments.inferno-21-g148: Libicocco is a proper name (subject). vegn’ is a contracted/poetic form of 'viene' (present tense) meaning 'comes' or 'comes forward' — the apostrophe marks elision. oltre functions adverbially here as 'further/forward' and e + Draghignazzo simply adds the coordinated name (and Draghignazzo) to the subject/verb phrase.
segments.inferno-21-g149: This is a list of names joined by repeated conjunctions. Cirïatto is followed by the adjective/epithet sannuto ('fanged'), placed after the name in typical poetic fashion; the repeated e's ('and') simply coordinate the names. The final adjective pazzo modifies Rubicante (an epithet: 'Rubicante the mad').
segments.inferno-21-g150: Cercate is the imperative plural 'search/look for' addressed to the devils. ’ntorno is a contracted form of 'intorno' meaning 'around'. Le boglienti pane is the direct object: the definite article + adjective (boglienti, 'boiling/seething') + pane (here 'pana' meaning sticky pitch), so 'the boiling pitch-pits'.
segments.inferno-21-g151: Costor is the demonstrative 'these fellows' (subject). Sian is the present subjunctive of 'essere' used optatively ('let them be' / 'may they be') and salvi is the predicate adjective 'safe/intact'. Infino a l’altro scheggio = 'up to/as far as the other rock/fragment' (infino … a + definite article); the relative clause che tutto intero va sovra le tane modifies 'scheggio' ('which goes entirely over the dens'), where va is present indicative 'goes/proceeds'.
segments.inferno-21-g152: Omè is an interjection of lament or surprise ('alas' / 'ah me').
segments.inferno-21-g153: Maestro is a vocative noun address ('master' or 'teacher'), here Dante calling Virgil his guide/teacher.
segments.inferno-21-g154: Che is an interrogative 'what' here; è is the present indicative 'is'; quel is the demonstrative 'that'; ch’ is the contracted relative/conjunction 'che' ('that'); i (here written with apostrophe) represents the enclitic form of the subject pronoun 'io' ('I') and veggio is first-person present 'I see' (poetic form of 'veggio' = 'vedo'). Altogether: 'what is that that I see?' Note: the listed senses for the token 'i' in the dataset do not match its use here (it is the pronoun 'I'), so no provided sense fits exactly.
segments.inferno-21-g155: This segment is a comma/punctuation mark introducing the speech; no lexical items to analyze.
segments.inferno-21-g156: Diss' io is an archaic/past form meaning 'I said' (diss' = poetic contraction of 'dissi', passato remoto); io is the first-person subject pronoun 'I' used for emphasis.
segments.inferno-21-g157: Deh is an exclamatory particle often rendered 'ah' or 'do come', used to urge or entreat.
segments.inferno-21-g158: Sanza is the preposition 'without'; scorta is the noun 'escort' or 'escort/guard' (object of sanza). Andianci is an archaic hortative/imperative first-person-plural form meaning 'let us go' ('andiamo' in modern Italian), and soli is 'alone' (adverb/adjective 'by ourselves').
segments.inferno-21-g159: Se is the conditional 'if' (introducing the clause); tu is the subject pronoun 'you'; sa’ is the second-person singular present of 'sapere' meaning 'know' specifically 'know how' here; ir is the infinitive 'to go'. The whole clause means 'if you know how to go'. (If the dataset's provided sense list for 'se' lacks 'if', no exact provided sense applies.)
segments.inferno-21-g160: Ch’ is the conjunction 'che' introducing the subordinate clause 'that I for my part do not ask it'. I (i’) is the pronoun 'I'; per me means 'for me' or 'as for me' (per + pronoun); me is the object/pronoun 'me/myself'; non is the negation 'not'; la in this context is a feminine clitic pronoun 'it/her' referring back to the escort (the dataset's listed senses for 'la' as an article do not match this pronominal use); cheggio is first-person present 'I ask/seek' (here 'I do not ask it for myself').
segments.inferno-21-g109: Per introduces a result/causal link ('wherefore/so'); mi mossi is passato remoto, reflexive, 'I moved / I set myself in motion'. e links the two actions; a lui venni places the prepositional phrase 'to him' before the verb venni (passato remoto of venire, 'I came'), and ratto is a postposed adverb meaning 'quickly'.
segments.inferno-21-g110: e introduces the clause; i diavoli is the subject 'the devils'. si fecer is the archaic passato remoto form of fare with reflexive si (fecero), meaning 'they made themselves' → 'they advanced'; tutti emphasizes 'all', and avanti is an adverb 'forward'.
segments.inferno-21-g111: sì ch’ introduces a result clause ('so that'); io temetti is passato remoto 'I feared'. In ch’ei tenesser patto the verb tenesser is an archaic subjunctive/conditional-form (equivalent to tenessero) after a verb of fearing, so it expresses the feared possibility 'that they might/ would keep (or have) a pact'.
segments.inferno-21-g112: così vid’ ïo is a contracted form of vidi io ('thus I saw'); the contraction vid' is passato remoto. già marks 'already'. temer here is an infinitive used as the complement of 'vidi' (Dante often uses 'see' + infinitive: 'I saw [them] to fear'), with li fanti as the direct object/subject of the infinitive; ch’uscivan is an imperfect relative clause 'who were coming out', and patteggiati is a past participle meaning 'having made terms / having negotiated'.
segments.inferno-21-g113: veggendo is a present gerund of vedere ('seeing'), linking the action to the preceding clause. sé is the reflexive pronoun 'themselves' referring back to li fanti; tra = 'among', nemici = 'enemies', and cotanti is an archaic adjective 'so many / so great', placed after the noun as was common in Dante.
segments.inferno-21-g114: I’ m’accostai is a contracted form (I’ = io) plus m’accostai, passato remoto reflexive 'I drew near / I came up' (accostarsi). con tutta la persona literally 'with the whole person' meaning 'with my whole body' (persona used for body). lungo ’l mio duca places the prepositional phrase 'along/beside my guide'—lungo + elided article '’l'—indicating proximity to the guide.
segments.inferno-21-g115: e non torceva li occhi: torceva (imperfect of torcere) here means 'avert' or 'turn aside' (one does not turn one's eyes away). li occhi is the direct object 'the eyes / gaze'. da la sembianza lor is a prepositional phrase 'from their appearance', and ch’era non buona is a relative clause with era (imperfect) 'which was not good/unpleasant'.
segments.inferno-21-g116: Ei chinavan is imperfect ('they were bending/they bent') with ei = archaic 'they'. li raffi is the object 'the hooks' or grappling-hooks. The short quoted question uses Vuo’ (contraction of vuoi) + che + subjunctive: 'Vuo’ che ’l tocchi' = 'Do you want (that) I touch it?' where tocchi is present subjunctive used because of che after a volitive verb.
segments.inferno-21-g117: diceva is imperfect 'was saying' or, with l’un con l’altro, functions idiomatically as 'one said to the other' (reciprocal exchange). l’un con l’altro literally 'one with the other' marks the reciprocal speech.
segments.inferno-21-g118: in sul is an archaic double preposition ('in' + 'sul' = 'on the'); groppone is an augmentative of groppa/groppo meaning 'big back' or 'big rump' (a coarse, physical descriptor). The phrase is the object-place of the verb in the quoted question.
segments.inferno-21-g119: This segment is the closing punctuation of the verse (a period).
segments.inferno-21-g94: Credi is a present indicative verb placed before the subject tu because this is a direct question; it functions here as 'do you think'. Tu is the stressed subject (second-person singular) placed after the verb for emphasis.
segments.inferno-21-g95: Malacoda is a vocative: direct address of the demon's name, set off by a comma; no grammatical change from nominative form.
segments.inferno-21-g96: Qui is an adverb 'here/at this place' modifying the embedded clause. Vedermi is the infinitive vedere + enclitic object mi ('to see/find me'); it begins an indirect infinitival clause dependent on credi. Esser venuto is a compound (perfect) infinitive, esse + past participle venuto, meaning 'to have come' — together: 'that I have come here.'
segments.inferno-21-g97: Disse is passato remoto (3rd sing.) 'he said' and functions as the speech tag. '’l' is the elided article il before a vowel; mio maestro is the noun phrase 'my master' (Virgil) in apposition to the speaker.
segments.inferno-21-g98: Sicuro già is an adverbial phrase: sicuro 'safe/secure' with già 'already' indicating prior safety. Da tutti vostri schermi is a prepositional phrase 'from/by all your shields' (schermi = defenses or shields), where da marks separation or protection from.
segments.inferno-21-g99: Sanza is the archaic form of senza 'without'. Voler divino is a noun phrase 'divine will' (voler = volere used as a noun); e connects to fato destro, where fato 'fate' and destro (here) means 'favorable' or 'right' — thus 'without divine will and favorable fate'. The whole is an interrogative continuation of the opening credi tu.
segments.inferno-21-g100: Lascian' is an archaic third-person plural form (used imperatively/subjunctively here) meaning 'let them' or 'allow them'; andar is the infinitive 'to go' — together 'let them go/pass.' The shortened apostrophe reflects epic/poetic elision.
segments.inferno-21-g101: Ché = perché 'for/because'. Nel cielo è voluto is an impersonal/passive periphrasis 'it has been willed in heaven' (è + past participle voluto). Ch’ i’ mostri... is an object clause introduced by che (here ch’ + i’ where ch’i’ = che io) with mostri as a verb (subjunctive) 'that I should show'; altrui = 'to others'; questo cammin silvestro = 'this wooded/wild path.' Note: i' here stands for io ('I'), and mostri is a verb form 'I show' in the subjunctive sense.
segments.inferno-21-g102: Allor = allora 'then'. Li here is a dative/clitic meaning 'to him' (archaic li = gli); fu is passato remoto of essere 'became/was'; l'orgoglio is 'the pride' as subject; sì caduto 'so fallen' — overall: 'then pride so fell upon him' (i.e., he was humbled).
segments.inferno-21-g103: Ch’ e’ (che e') introduces a result clause: 'so that'. Si lasciò is a reflexive/permissive use of lasciare in passato remoto 'he let himself/allowed' and cascar (infinitive) means 'to fall'; l’uncino is 'the hook' and a’ piedi 'at his feet' marks the location where the hook fell.
segments.inferno-21-g104: E disse = 'and he said'. A li altri = 'to the others', where li is the (archaic) article/pronoun used with altri 'others' (word order a + article + noun). This introduces direct speech.
segments.inferno-21-g105: Omai is an adverb 'henceforth/now on'; non sia is negative present subjunctive of essere 'may he not be' (a hortatory/permission clause); feruto is a past participle 'wounded/struck' — together: a command or wish that he not be struck henceforth.
segments.inferno-21-g106: E = 'and' linking speech. '’l duca mio' = 'my duke/guide' with the elided article. A me is the indirect object 'to me' — this is the speech tag (he addressed me).
segments.inferno-21-g107: O tu che siedi = 'O you who sit' (vocative + relative clause). Tra li scheggion del ponte = 'among the jutting/splintered stones of the bridge' (tra + plural article + noun phrase). Quatto quatto is a reduplicated adverbial expression meaning 'very quietly/stealthily' (quatto = stealthy/crouching). Note: siedi is used as 'sit' in modern sense despite the lemma given; the clause describes the addressee's posture.
segments.inferno-21-g108: Sicuramente = 'safely/certainly'. Omai = 'now/henceforth'. A me ti riedi is an imperative or hortatory second-person singular with clitic ti 'to yourself/you' + riedi (riedare 'return') meaning 'return to me' or 'come back to my side'.
segments.inferno-21-g161: Se introduces a conditional 'if'. tu is the explicit subject 'you'. se’ is the archaic/poetic contraction of sei (2nd sing. present of essere) meaning 'you are'. sì is an adverbial intensifier 'so/very' modifying accorto; accorto is a past-participle/adjectival use of accorgere meaning 'aware/alert'. come suoli is a comparative clause 'as you are accustomed' where suoli is an archaic present form of solere ('be wont to'); the whole phrase = 'if you are as alert as you usually are'.
segments.inferno-21-g162: non vedi tu is a negative question 'do you not see' with non + present ved(i). ch’ is the subordinating che 'that' introducing the content clause; e links the two predicates. digrignan is 3rd‑person plural present (poetic) of digrignare 'gnash/bare (their) teeth'. li denti is the direct object 'the teeth'. e con le ciglia ne minaccian duoli: con + noun expresses the instrument ('with their eyelashes/brows'); ne is a clitic pronoun referring back (roughly 'of them / by them'); minaccian is 3rd‑pl. present 'they threaten' and duoli is the object 'sorrows/pains'.
segments.inferno-21-g163: A single sentence that consists of the terminal punctuation (a period) indicating the close of the quoted question; there are no lexical items to analyze.
segments.inferno-21-g164: Ed is the emphatic form of e 'and'. elli here is the subject pronoun (archaic spelling of egli) meaning 'he' (the speaker); a me is a prepositional phrase 'to me' indicating the indirect object/recipient of the speech. Together this is the speech tag 'And he to me:'.
segments.inferno-21-g165: Non vo’ che tu paventi: non + vo' (vo’ is a contracted/poetic form; here = voglio 'I want' rather than 'I go') gives 'I do not want'. che introduces a subordinate clause with tu paventi (subjunctive/optative nuance in context) meaning 'that you be afraid' — here rendered 'I don't want you to be afraid'.
segments.inferno-21-g166: lasciali is the imperative/infinitival idea 'let them' or 'leave them' (lascia + li) meaning 'allow them to...'; digrignar is the infinitive 'to gnash' (used as the verb of the let-phrase). pur is an adverb 'still/even/indeed' adding insistence; a lor senno = 'to their judgment/mind' (a + lor + senno) meaning 'as they see fit' or 'to their own sense'.
segments.inferno-21-g167: ch’ is causal/relative 'for/because' here; e is simply 'and'; fanno is 3rd‑person plural present 'they do' or 'they make'; ciò 'this' is the demonstrative object; per introduces the reason 'because of/for'; li lessi is a plural nominal phrase (lessi = boiled/exhausted/boiled ones in the supplied senses — a specific local term here) and dolenti modifies it 'sorrowful/painful'. The clause as a whole: 'for they do this because of the sorrowful lessi.'
segments.inferno-21-g168: Per l'argine sinistro is a prepositional phrase 'along/along the left bank' (per + definite article + noun + adjective). volta dienno is a compact, somewhat archaic expression: volta 'a turn/turning' and dienno is an archaic 3rd‑person plural past form meaning 'they gave/they made' — together 'they turned (about) the left bank'.
segments.inferno-21-g169: ma prima aveva ciascun la lingua stretta coi denti: ma 'but'; prima 'before/first'; aveva is imperfect 'had' (imperfect of avere) describing a prior state; ciascun 'each one'; la lingua stretta 'the tongue tight' = 'the tongue clenched'; coi denti = con + i (with the) 'between/against the teeth'. The whole describes a habitual/past posture each held before turning.
segments.inferno-21-g170: verso lor duca: verso 'toward' indicates direction; lor is the clitic for 'their/them'; duca here is 'leader/guide' (Dante uses duca for 'guide'). Grammatically this is a prepositional phrase 'toward their leader' explaining the direction of the sign.
segments.inferno-21-g171: per cenno: per 'by/with' introducing the means; cenno 'a sign/nod' — 'as a sign' or 'by way of a sign'. This short phrase modifies the previous action (the tongue clenched was done as a sign).
segments.inferno-21-g172: ed elli aveva del cul fatto trombetta: ed 'and'; elli (here an archaic/poetic subject form) = 'he/that one' (the leader just mentioned); aveva is imperfect 'had made/was having'; del cul = di + il 'of the rump/bottom'; fatto is the past participle 'made' used adjectivally; trombetta 'a little trumpet' — literally 'and he had made a little trumpet of his buttocks', i.e. fashioned it into a trumpet-like sound-producer.
segments.inferno-21-g132: E se = 'And if', a simple conjunction + conditional subordinating particle. l’andare is the infinitive used as a noun ('the going' / 'going on'); avante is a postpositive adverb modifying andare ('forward' / 'onward'). pur is an emphatic adverb meaning 'still' / 'even so'. vi piace is an impersonal/psychological construction: piacere takes an indirect-object pronoun (vi = 'to you') and piacere is 3rd-person singular 'it pleases / you like'. Note the elision l’ before a vowel.
segments.inferno-21-g133: andatevene is an emphatic plural imperative built from andare + enclitic pronouns (andate + ve + ne) meaning 'go away / go off'. su per = 'up through' (su = up; per = through/along). questa grotta: questa agrees in gender with grotta; grotta = 'grotto/cave'.
segments.inferno-21-g134: presso is an adverb meaning 'near/close by' and Dante places it before the verb (presso è) for poetic inversion (adverb + verb + subject). un altro scoglio is the subject ('another rock/reef'). che introduces a relative clause; via is the object 'a way/path', and face is an archaic/literary 3rd-person form of fare ('makes/forms') — a verb form not represented in the supplied noun senses.
segments.inferno-21-g135: Ier is an archaic adverb meaning 'yesterday' (used alone as a time adverb).
segments.inferno-21-g136: più oltre = 'more beyond' / 'further on'; cinqu’ is the elided form of cinque (elision of the final vowel before a following vowel or consonant), ore = 'hours'. che here introduces a temporal clause roughly 'since/after/than' and quest’ otta is an archaic phrasing for 'this eighth (hour)' (quest’ = questa elided; otta = forma arcaica di otto/ottava). The whole phrase gives a temporal marker 'more than five hours past this eighth.'
segments.inferno-21-g137: mille dugento con sessanta sei anni compié is a time formula: 'one thousand two hundred with sixty-six years completed' — Dante uses con (= 'and/with') to link the hundreds and the units. compié is the passato remoto 3rd-singular of compiere used impersonally ('were completed'). che introduces the clause of reference/when: qui la via fu rotta = 'here the road was broken' where fu (passato remoto of essere) is auxiliary 'was' and rotta is the past participle agreeing with la via (feminine). Note: several supplied sense lists for numerals or participles are mismatched, so principal grammatical roles are as described.
segments.inferno-21-g138: Io mando is a first-person present 'I send' (mando + infinitive indicates sending someone to do something). Verso là = 'toward there/that direction'; di questi miei = 'of these (my) [men]' (my followers). A riguardar is an infinitive of purpose 'to look/inspect.' The clause s’alcun se ne sciorina is conditional: s’ is a contracted particle (se = 'if'), alcun = 'anyone', se ne sciorina = 'withdraws himself from it/leaves his post' where ne is a pronominal particle 'from there/of it' and sciorina (sciorinare) is present indicative describing the possible action checked for.
segments.inferno-21-g139: Gite is the plural imperative 'go (you plural)' (from gire/girare or in usage 'andate'); con lor = 'with them' (con + archaic object pronoun lor). The short command sends the addressees along with those men.
segments.inferno-21-g140: Che introduces the clause of result/explanation 'that', and non saranno rei is future indicative (saranno = 'they will be') with non negating it; rei here means 'guilty/culpable' (i.e. 'you will not be held guilty'). The future is used to reassure or promise that following the order will not make them culpable.
segments.inferno-21-g141: Tra’ti is a contracted/poetic phrasing equivalent to tra te (literally “among/between you”) followed by avante (“forward/advance”). The phrase is an instruction to the addressees to move forward; the preposition+clitic contraction and the postposed adverb are typical of older/poetic Italian word order.
segments.inferno-21-g142: Alichino is a vocative: the speaker is addressing the demon by name; the comma marks the direct address.
segments.inferno-21-g143: e = “and,” linking the next name; Calcabrina is another vocative (name). The closing quotation mark after the name shows this is part of direct speech.
segments.inferno-21-g144: cominciò is passato remoto (third person singular) of cominciare: “he began.” EllI here is the subject pronoun (an emphatic/poetic form of egli, “he”) placed after the verb for stylistic emphasis; a introduces the infinitive dire, so cominciò a dire = “he began to say.”
segments.inferno-21-g145: e tu = “and you,” continuing the direct address and shifting to another individual; tu is vocative/second-person target of the command.
segments.inferno-21-g146: Cagnazzo is another vocative (the name of a demon); the semicolon indicates a pause before the next clause of the direct command.
segments.inferno-21-g147: This clause gives the final part of the command. e = “and.” Barbariccia is the subject in direct address. Guidi here is not a simple present indicative of a proper name but functions as the verb ‘to guide/lead’ in a subjunctive/jussive sense (a directive: “let Barbariccia lead” / “Barbariccia, lead”), a common medieval/poetic device for issuing orders; la decina means “the group of ten” (the squad of ten demons).

sentences.inferno-21-s6: Then I turned like a man who is slow to see what he must flee and whose sudden fear weakens him; who, in order to see, does not delay in departing: and I saw behind us a black devil coming, running up the crag.

sentences.inferno-21-s18: My good master said to me, 'So that it won't appear that you are here,' and he added, 'crouch down behind a splinter of rock so that you may have some shelter; and, lest any offence be done to me, do not be afraid, for I have matters settled — I have been in such a bargain before.'
sentences.inferno-21-s19: Then he crossed from that side of the bridge; and when he reached up to the sixth bank, he found it necessary to keep a steady front.
sentences.inferno-21-s20: With that fury and with that tempest which dogs rush upon the poor fellow — who at once asks where he should stop — those from beneath the little bridge came out, and they turned all their hooks against him; but he cried, "Let none of you be a villain!"
sentences.inferno-21-s1: Thus from bridge to bridge, talking of other things that my Comedy does not care to sing, we came; and we kept the crest, when we stopped to look at the other fissure of Malebolge and the other vain laments; and I saw it marvelously dark.
sentences.inferno-21-s2: As in the Venetian arsenal in winter the tenacious pitch boils to recaulk their unsound timbers—because they cannot sail—in that place some make a new hull and some recaulk the ribs of the one that has made many voyages; some hammer from the prow and some from the stern; others make oars and others handle the rigging; some patch the small sail and the mizzen—such, not by fire but by divine craft, boiled below a thick lump of pitch that stuck to the bank on every side.
sentences.inferno-21-s3: I saw it, but in it I never saw anything except the bubbles that the boiling raised, swelling up entirely and then settling back compressed.
sentences.inferno-21-s4: While I was fixedly gazing down there, my guide, saying, 'Look, look!'
sentences.inferno-21-s33: And they answered: “Yes — make him set it on him.”

sentences.inferno-21-s36: Then he said to us: “You cannot go any farther over this rock, because the sixth arch lies completely broken at the bottom.”

sentences.inferno-21-s25: “Do you think, Malacoda, that I have come here?” said my master, “safe already from all your shields, without divine will and favorable fate?”
sentences.inferno-21-s26: “Let them go,” he said, “for it has been willed in Heaven that I should show others this wooded path.”

sentences.inferno-21-s28: And my duke to me: “O you who sit among the jutting stones of the bridge, crouching so stealthily, certainly now return to me.”

sentences.inferno-21-s38: Yesterday, more than five hours past this eighth, one thousand two hundred and sixty-six years were completed since the road here was broken.
sentences.inferno-21-s39: I send some of my men over there to see whether any of them loosens himself; go with them, for you will not be guilty.
sentences.inferno-21-s40: “Between you, advance, Alichino and Calcabrina,” he began to say, “and you, Cagnazzo; and Barbariccia, lead the ten.”
